seo_title: Kalman Evolve: Closing the Gap in Kalman Filtering via Interpretable…
meta_description: Discover how Kalman Evolve improves state estimation by optimizing both noise parameters and update structure, achieving up to 12% reduction in RMSE.
body_html:
The Kalman Evolve framework improves state estimation by jointly optimizing both noise parameters and update structure, achieving up to 12% reduction in RMSE.

Improving State Estimation with Kalman Evolve

Optimizing both noise parameters and update structure leads to improved state estimation results. The approach leverages large language models as a structured prior over program space, enabling the generation of interpretable, non-affine modifications to the classical Kalman filter. The discovered algorithms consistently improve over strong baselines such as the Optimized Kalman Filter, achieving up to 12% reduction in RMSE.

Interpretable updates The approach generates interpretable, non-affine modifications to the classical Kalman filter.

Improved performance Kalman Evolve achieves up to 12% reduction in RMSE compared to strong baselines.

Robustness to nonlinearity The approach is robust to nonlinear systems, addressing a common challenge in state estimation.

Real-World Applications of Kalman Evolve Kalman Evolve has been successfully applied to various real-world tracking benchmarks, including Doppler radar, LiDAR-based localization, and pedestrian tracking.

source.title: The Kalman Evolve: Closing the Gap in Kalman Filtering via Interpretable Algorithm Discovery
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new framework called Kalman Evolve to address the limitations of traditional Kalman filtering algorithms in real-world sensing settings. The proposed approach uses large language models to generate improved filtering algorithms that can handle nonlinear dynamics and noise, leading to better performance than existing methods. Through experiments on various synthetic and real-world tracking benchmarks, the researchers have shown that Kalman Evolve can achieve significant improvements over strong baselines, with some algorithms reducing root mean square error (RMSE) by up to 12%.
